content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 16, 1991


Susan Bott
P.O. Box 2483
Goleta, CA  93118

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-91-386

Dear Ms. Bott:

     This letter confirms the advice I provided to you on
August 7, 1991, concerning the formation of a controlled committee
for election to the Goleta Water District Board of Directors which
will be jointly controlled by multiple candidates.  You stated
that such committees were authorized by the water district.
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to be
opinion concerning the local election laws of the Goleta Water
District.  The Commission's jurisdiction is limited to the
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

     As I stated in our telephone conversation, prior to the
enactment of Proposition 73 in 1988, joint candidate committees
were permitted under the Act.  However, with the adoption of
Proposition 73 and the transfer ban of Section 85304, joint
candidate committees became impermissible.  (See, Roberti Advice
Letter, No. A-89-125, copy enclosed.)

     On September 25, 1990, a United States District Court issued
an order in Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al.
v. Fair Political Practices Commission (1990) 747 F.Supp. 580
("SEIU") which became effective on January 18, 1991 and which
invalidated provisions of the Act added by Proposition 73 in June
of 1988.  These provisions included the fiscal year contribution


1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

Our File No. A-91-386
Page 2


limitations of the Act and the transfer ban of Section 85304.²
(See, Dorman Advice Letter, No. I-91-253, copy enclosed.)
Consequently, joint committees are no longer prohibited.

     Please note that section 85201 continues to require that all
contributions or loans made to a candidate, or to the candidate's
controlled committee shall be deposited in a single campaign bank
account established for the specific election.  The Commission has
interpreted this to mean that a candidate for elective office may
have only one campaign bank account and controlled committee for
each election. (Regulation 18521.)³  Thus if you establish a joint
campaign committee for the Goleta Water District election, you may
not have any other controlled committee for that election, and all
contributions or loans made to you or your controlled committee
for that election shall be deposited in the joint campaign bank
account.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                              Sincerely,

                              Scott Hallabrin
                              Acting General Counsel

                              By: John W. Wallace
                                  Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa


___________________________
     ² Please be aware that the SEIU decision has been appealed.
Moreover, the court order did not invalidate the special election
and special runoff election contribution limits of Proposition 73
or the transfer ban of Section 85304 as applied to prohibit
candidates for state or local office from transferring campaign
funds to any candidate who is subject to any valid contribution
limits. You cited that candidates in the Goleta Water District
election were not subject to contribution limits and that the
election was not a special election.

     ³ A person running for two separate elective offices may
establish one campaign bank account and one controlled committee
for each office sought. (Regulation 18520(c).)

sections.question_synthetic: Are joint candidate committees permitted under California Political Reform Act for a Goleta Water District Board of Directors election after Proposition 73 and the SEIU court decision?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Joint candidate committees are now permitted following the SEIU v. FPPC court decision that invalidated certain Proposition 73 provisions, but candidates may only have one controlled committee per election with a single campaign bank account.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a response letter to Susan Bott. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections with headers; extracted content from body text. Letter references enclosed Advice Letters A-89-125 and I-91-253. Contains footnotes with additional legal context about SEIU appeal and special election limits.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are joint candidate committees permitted under California Political Reform Act for a Goleta Water District Board of Directors election after Proposition 73 and the SEIU court decision?

CONCLUSION: Joint candidate committees are now permitted following the SEIU v. FPPC court decision that invalidated certain Proposition 73 provisions, but candidates may only have one controlled committee per election with a single campaign bank account.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to inquiry about forming a jointly-controlled candidate committee for Goleta Water District Board elections. The letter explains that while Proposition 73 initially prohibited joint committees, the SEIU court decision invalidated those restrictions, making joint committees permissible again, subject to the requirement of a single campaign bank account per election.
